narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, we know how much heart and determination it takes to pursue this path, and we're here to help you understand the automated decision on your application in a clear way. Your profile includes Year 1 school grades placing you in the 2nd decile, Year 3 grades in the 1st decile, a middle family income bracket, an LSAT score of 25.50, an undergraduate GPA of 3.00, and plans to attend full-time—details the model compared against thousands of past students' records. The model predicts a 63.2% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is why your application was rejected: law schools use this prediction to prioritize applicants likely to pass and succeed in practice. The biggest factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking, as being in the bottom of your class there strongly signaled higher risk compared to typical students who rank much higher on average. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA worked in your favor, pulling the prediction toward passing since your 3.00 is close to the average and shows solid college performance. Your LSAT score had a moderate influence toward failure, as your 25.50 is notably below the typical 36.70 seen in successful students. Other elements like your middle family income and full-time plans slightly supported a passing prediction, while your Year 1 decile had minimal impact. Overall, your Year 3 performance stood out as the most influential red flag when stacked against peers who passed the bar, despite some strengths in your GPA and background. This isn't a judgment on your potential—it's just how your specifics compared to historical patterns. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your recent grades through additional coursework or prep programs tailored for underrepresented students like yourself, and reapplying with those improvements to shift future predictions. Many have turned similar profiles around, and resources like bar prep scholarships for Black women can make a real difference. We're rooting for you—keep pushing.
